DS Analysis: Despite a weak pass-catching corps, Allen led all QBs in fantasy points per game in 2025. At 30, Allen’s run of elite play should continue for the foreseeable future. He gets a nice boost with the addition of D.J. Moore, who should stick around for at least the next two seasons.

Mahomes posted underwhelming production in 2024, ultimately finishing as the QB11. When healthy in 2025, he lacked consistency from week to week. Instead, he relied on rushing stats to finish as a top-5 fantasy QB pre-injury. Unfortunately, he suffered a December ACL/LCL tear that puts his availability for Week 1, 2026, in question.

Burrow performed as a high-end QB1 in 2024. He was also one of the offseason winners in 2025, as Cincy retained WRs Ja’Marr Chase AND Tee Higgins on long-term deals. Then came a disastrous '25 season that was thrown off track by a Week 2 turf toe injury. Fortunately, Burrow returned and flashed his high-end upside on a couple of occasions. He should have multiple years of top-8 production remaining.

Jackson posted an MVP-caliber 2024, resulting in a career-high 488 fantasy points. But injuries to his back and hamstring severely limited his fantasy output in 2025. In fact, Jackson delivered only three top-5 fantasy finishes -- each over the first three weeks of the season. Jackson is a bounce-back candidate for 2026, his age-29 season.

Well-built at 6’4, 230, Maye showed he could make any throw on the field as a rookie. Then, alongside an upgraded O-line and an improved WR corps, Maye’s play reached elite levels in 2025. He ranked top five in passing TDs, passing yards, yards per attempt, and passing success rate. His arrow is pointing straight up ahead of Year 3.

Daniels proved pro-ready as a rookie, throwing for 3,568 yards, 25 TDs, and 9 INTs on a 69% completion rate. His 891 rushing yards ranked second among QBs. Washington added useful pieces in the '25 offseason, including WR Deebo Samuel and LT Laremy Tunsil, but a pair of elbow injuries ultimately derailed the QB's season. At only 25, Daniels is a prime rebound candidate for 2026 and beyond.

With OC Arthur Smith out of town, Robinson's production exploded to 1,881 total yards and 15 TDs in 2024. While his TD count dropped in 2025, he still posted a career-high 22.0 PPR points per game. He also played all 17 games for the third straight season. At only 24, Bijan should remain an elite fantasy option for the foreseeable future.

Alongside new HC Jim Harbaugh, WR Ladd McConkey, and LT Joe Alt, Herbert played excellent real-life ball in 2024. However, he finished as a QB1 in only five of 17 games. The team added back Keenan Allen in 2025, plus spent early picks on RB Omarion Hampton and WR Tre Harris. The team even saw a quick rise from Round 5 TE Oronde Gadsden II. Herbert ultimately delivered a fine fantasy season with help from boosted rushing volume. But an injury-ravaged O-line and another Herbert injury -- this time to his hand -- limited what could have been an even better season. With plenty of talent around him, Herbert remains an upside QB1 alongside new OC Mike McDaniel.

Despite a slow start, Gibbs largely delivered as a rookie RB. He averaged 5.2 YPC and popped in 10 TDs on 182 attempts, adding 52 receptions. His production reached new heights in 2024, and he proved more than capable as a workhorse back following a late-season injury to David Montgomery. 2025 turned up much of the same, as he finished as the PPR RB3 even with a few late-season duds. The former Round 1 pick is now eligible for a contract extension.

Love was a force at Notre Dame, posting back-to-back 1,100-yard rushing seasons with 35 total rushing TDs. His contact balance and power rank among the best RB traits in recent draft classes, and his profile stacks up favorably with Ashton Jeanty’s. Arizona doesn't supply an excellent situation in 2026, as the OL and QB spots lack talent. Still, Love brings true three-down upside and will be just 21 years old as a rookie. He's an easy fit inside the top-five dynasty RBs.

Chase took a slight step back after his 2024 triple crown, but Joe Burrow’s extended absence shaped much of that dip. Even so, Chase still finished WR3 in PPR points per game at 19.7. That number felt much closer to his floor than his ceiling. As long as Burrow stays healthy, Chase will push for the top WR spot each year for the foreseeable future. He's only 26 years old.

Hampton carried the North Carolina offense for two years -- 2023-2024 -- including one year when Drake Maye was the QB. The former four-star recruit tallied two straight years of 1,500+ rushing yards and 15 TDs, and he adds solid receiving potential. A shifty runner who can hit the home run, Hampton showed his athleticism by crushing the Combine with a 4.48 40-yard dash and a 38" vertical. While he landed in a dreamy spot with HC Jim Harbaugh and the Chargers, Hampton made only nine appearances due to an ankle fracture. If healthy, an RB1 season is in reach for '26 and beyond.

Hurts set a career-high in passing TDs in 2025. However, his rushing production took a hit, leading to a career low in fantasy points per game. Hurts did manage to avoid missing time after a concussion cost him two games in 2024. He enters next season likely to be without top WR A.J. Brown, although Philly added a versatile pass catcher in Round 1 WR Makai Lemon. Hurts is under contract through the 2028 season.

A shoulder injury limited Lawrence to only 10 games in 2024. Then in 2025, he rebounded tremendously under new HC Liam Coen. Lawrence tallied 4,007-29-12 -- plus nine rushing TDs. That propelled him to an overall QB4 fantasy finish. Lawrence's huge season came despite only seven games alongside rookie WR Travis Hunter. We also saw WR Brian Thomas Jr. take a major step back. Veteran Jakobi Meyers stepped up following a mid-season trade, as the team extended him through the 2028 season. Regardless of the pieces, Lawrence's stock is clearly on the rise.

Jeanty went from a 1,900-yard sophomore season to a 2,700-yard junior season. His college tape showed elite contact balance, burst, and receiving ability. Arriving as the sixth overall pick, Jeanty showed elite flashes, most notably in a Week 16 game at Houston. Overall, Jeanty's effectiveness was limited by poor O-line and QB play. 2026 will bring major offensive changes, although Jeanty still projects as a workhorse.

Collins finished WR11 in PPR points per game in 2025 (15.1). Houston's struggles on offense made for a volatile season, but Collins came on late with C.J. Stroud and Davis Mills. He was a top-12 WR in a struggling offense, so the potential for even better results exists over the next few years. Collins is under contract through the 2027 season.

JSN took another step forward in his third season, finishing as WR2 in PPR points per game (21.3). Even on a Seahawks offense that wanted to lean on the run, JSN remained dominant. His route running and athleticism leave him open on most passing plays, which led to 163 targets from Sam Darnold. At only 24 years old, JSN is one of the top players in dynasty.

The 2025 season marked a low point for Jefferson, at least by his standards. He finished WR31 in PPR points per game at 11.9 while dealing with a three-man QB rotation of JJ McCarthy, Carson Wentz, and Max Brosmer. Jefferson still reached 1,000 receiving yards again, stretching that streak to seven seasons. He remains in the thick of his prime. Plus, Kyler Murray arrived in March to bolster the QB room.

Nacua delivered a career year in 2025, finishing as the PPR WR1 with 377 fantasy points. Matthew Stafford fed him 166 targets, making Nacua the clear centerpiece of the Rams’ passing game. At 24, he remains locked in as one of dynasty’s top WRs. The one thing keeping him from the top overall spot is the long-term uncertainty at QB.

Lamb shared the spotlight with George Pickens, but he was still productive. He finished 2025 as WR10 in PPR points per game at 15.5. Lamb did have a short stint on IR after an AC joint sprain, but he remains an upside fantasy asset when healthy. At only 26, Lamb should be a fantasy mainstay for a long time.

Henderson was highly recruited and showed top-end traits at Ohio State, particularly his burst and pass-catching ability. He ran as expected at the Combine with a 4.43 40-yard dash and tied for second among RBs with a 1.52 10-yard split. Henderson appeared in all 17 games as a rookie but wasn't a major contributor until midseason. (He finished as a top-25 PPR back in seven of his final nine games.) An elite QB and an improved O-line only add value here.

Williams showed the ability to make high-end throws as a rookie. But his tendency to hold the ball and take sacks led to a wildly inconsistent campaign. Fortunately, an improved O-line and an upgraded pass-catching corps arrived last offseason and immediately paid dividends. TE Colston Loveland and WR Luther Burden really showed promise and supply long-term building blocks. Williams should only improve as he gains time in HC Ben Johnson's system.

Taylor missed three games in 2024 but remained busy on a per-game basis, averaging 23 touches. The former Round 2 pick then went off for a HUGE 2025 that included 323 carries, 1,585 yards, and 18 TDs. He added a career-high 46 catches and 378 yards. Now, Taylor's huge career workload makes him a candidate for an early decline. In the short-term, though, this Indy offense should remain centered on the run. JT is signed for one more season.

Bowers disappointed in 2025 while dealing with a knee injury. He only played in 12 games and finished with 64-680-7 receiving. The talent is still undeniable, and Bowers should rebound alongside Fernando Mendoza in 2026.

Achane's rushing efficiency hit a snag in 2024. But his receiving work went through the roof: 78 catches, 592 yards, and 6 TDs. Another easy RB1 season surfaced in 2025, although he'll now hit Year 4 alongside a new QB and HC -- and a rebuilding roster.

McBride had a dominant 2025, breaking the single-season TE receptions record in Week 17. His fantasy season ended at 119-1174-11. QB play is a minor issue in the short term, but there's no doubt McBride remains an elite dynasty TE.

The Lions faltered in 2025, but St. Brown kept producing. He ranked fourth in PPR points per game at 19.1. He also ranked second in the league in targets at 172. St. Brown and Jared Goff are both signed through the 2028 season.

A 25-target start over the first two games hinted at a huge 2025 for Nabers. Then a torn ACL cut the year short. The injury could cap his early-season impact in 2026, but the long-term dynasty outlook still looks strong. Nabers remains a premium talent tied to rising QB Jaxson Dart.

London started the season hot but missed four games with a PCL strain. He still ended as the WR8 in PPR points per game at 16.8. Atlanta's current WR corps suggests another high-volume role in 2026. Only 25, London projects as a long-term WR1.

Loveland flashed elite potential in 2025, especially after a late-season injury to Rome Odunze. With DJ Moore off to Buffalo, Loveland's role should only grow in 2026 and beyond. He'll play his second season at only 22 years old.

Purdy missed a chunk of early-season games with turf toe. But he rebounded to finish as the QB4 in total points from Week 11 on. Purdy benefits from HC stability and a solid group of pass catchers, although Christian McCaffrey and George Kittle are likely nearing the end of their prime. San Francisco also added veteran Mike Evans on a multi-year deal. Purdy is under contract through the 2030 season.

Dart flashed his arm talent as a true freshman at USC, but he was fully unlocked in 2024 at Ole Miss. He completed 69.3% of his passes for 4,279 yards, 29 TDs, and 6 INTs. With a quick release and improved coverage recognition, Dart quickly showed the potential to stick as an NFL starter. He also adds plenty of mobility to make a fantasy impact moving forward. While durability issues persist, Dart certainly carries top-10 potential if the Giants continue upgrading the offense.

Warren's 2025 was a tale of two seasons. He dominated for the first half of the year, achieving top-5 TE numbers in PPR. But an injury to Daniel Jones led to an awful late-season surge for the rookie. Zooming out, the future still looks bright here, especially after the trade of Michael Pittman. Warren should be a featured part of the passing game moving forward.

Fannin not only took over the No. 1 TE spot -- he became the No. 1 offensive weapon for the Browns. He ended the 2025 fantasy season 72-731-6 on 107 targets, and that was playing with three different QBs. He enters 2026 at age 22 with his best days ahead.

As an older prospect at 24, Nix proved pro-ready alongside HC Sean Payton. Across 17 games, he tallied 3,775-29-12 -- plus 430-4 rushing. The 2025 offseason brought the arrival of veteran TE Evan Engram and Round 3 WR Pat Bryant, along with a new deal for Courtland Sutton. While Nix's play proved inconsistent, he still finished QB7 on a league-high 612 attempts. He also added 5 rushing TDs and finished tied for fifth in QB rush attempts. 2026 turned up a trade for Jaylen Waddle, giving Nix one of the best WR corps league-wide.

McMillan enjoyed a successful first season, finishing as the WR23 in fantasy points per game (12.6). The Panthers went run-heavy under QB Bryce Young, who struggled to provide consistency in the passing game. Still, McMillan was the clear No. 1 for a team short on receiving depth. He flashed excellent body control and improved as the season went on. There is star-level, top-tier potential with McMillan, who's only 23 years old.

Rice started 2025 with a six-game suspension and ended the season on IR with a concussion. In his eight games, he was consistently targeted by Patrick Mahomes, finishing as the WR5 in PPR points per game (18.5). Outside of injury risk, Rice appears to be the clear No. 1 WR on the Chiefs, and the scheme has been built to utilize his talents. We'll see what comes of his off-field issues, which include domestic abuse allegations.

Cook erupted for a massive 18 total TDs in 2024. He posted another 14 scores this past fall, also supplying a career-high 1,621 rushing yards in 17 games. He inked a contract extension last August, keeping him under team control through the 2029 season.

Coming off his best season (93-1,429-9), the Cowboys applied the franchise tag to Pickens in February. A long-term extension in Dallas might be the best-case scenario for Pickens' dynasty value. Just entering his prime, Pickens is an ascending talent with a ton of fantasy appeal.

Dak’s 2024 was ruined by a hamstring injury that required surgery. But he rebounded with a healthy 2025 to post top-five fantasy totals (4,552 yards, 30 TDs, 10 INTs). We'll see about the status of George Pickens, but expect Dallas to retain him.

Egbuka got off to a hot start, averaging 16.1 PPR points per game and ranking inside the top-10 WRs in the first 10 weeks of his rookie season. However, he would only top 50 yards once and didn't score a TD in Tampa's final eight games. Those first 10 games show Egbuka's potential. With Mike Evans leaving as a free agent, Egbuka will be in a position to open his sophomore campaign as the Buccaneers' No. 1 WR. While Baker Mayfield returns, the Bucs brought in a new OC in Zac Robinson.

Kraft enjoyed a strong 2025 before a November ACL tear cost him his season. He ended the year going 32-489-6 in only 8 games. He not only flashed good hands, but Kraft also showed after-the-catch ability in his third season. He remains a young TE on an offense that loves to use the position. The 25-year-old is a top dynasty TE moving forward.

Walker encountered an up-and-down regular season in 2025, limited by Zach Charbonnet's goal line role. When Charbonnet suffered a torn ACL in the postseason, Walker stepped up for a productive run to the Super Bowl. This culminated in a 161-yard, MVP performance. Walker chose an ideal landing spot in free agency, joining a Chiefs offense that no longer rosters Kareem Hunt or Isiah Pacheco.

Stroud's fantasy points per game rebounded slightly from 2024 (18.2 vs. 17.0). Still, he wasn't asked to carry a team that fielded one of the NFL's top defenses. That played a part in Stroud posting only three top-12 fantasy finishes in 14 games. Nico Collins, Jayden Higgins, and Jaylin Noel supply a nice group of young weapons. Still, a lack of rushing production -- plus middling pass volume -- figures to stick around moving forward.

In 2025, Love missed two games for the second straight season. When healthy, he flashed with three overall QB2 fantasy finishes. However, he tallied only one other finish inside the top-10. Overall, Love’s volume continued to stay low relative to his breakout 2023. Green Bay also remains without a true No. 1 WR, although it’s a huge positive that Christian Watson has looked strong in his return from an ACL tear. Romeo Doubs joined New England in free agency, putting more pressure on Matthew Golden's development. Love is under contract through the 2028 season.

A largely ineffective Aaron Rodgers limited Hall in 2024, while the RB also battled through a knee injury. 2025 turned up even worse QB play, although Hall still posted a career-high 1,065 rushing yards. He returned to the Jets via the franchise tag, while his situation improved a bit with the arrival of Geno Smith. Still, it's reasonable to expect Hall to play elsewhere in 2027 and beyond.

CMC was excellent in 2023, finishing as the clear-cut RB1. Then came a nightmare 2024 that included multiple lower-body injuries (and only 50 total carries). He rebounded to a PPR RB1 finish in 2025, boosted by a huge 102 catches. Of equal importance: CMC played all 17 games. He'll play next season at 30, signaling some dynasty risk.

Judkins answered questions about his athleticism with a 4.48 40-yard dash at the NFL Combine and tied for second among RBs with a 1.52 10-yard split. His rookie season started nicely, but he was ultimately limited by a poor supporting cast. Judkins' season also ended with a Week 16 fibula fracture and ankle dislocation. Following surgery, he's expected to be ready for training camp, where he should retain lead-back work.

Pitts rebounded in 2025, posting career highs in targets (118), catches (88), and TDs (5). He also played all 17 games for the third straight season. Atlanta placed the franchise tag on him over the winter, and we'll see if both sides can agree on a long-term deal.

For only the second time in his career, Olave made it through 16 games. He capitalized with career highs in catches, yards, and TDs, finishing with a 100-1,163-9 line in Kellen Moore’s offense. The late-season QB play mattered, too: all three of his 100-yard games came with Tyler Shough under center. The NFL draft brought top-10 pick Jordyn Tyson, a player capable of limiting Olave's target ceiling right away. Just note that the veteran is scheduled to enter a contract season.

2025 was another strong season for Higgins. He topped double-digit TDs for the second consecutive year and ranked as the WR16, averaging 14.0 PPR points per game. However, he missed three games with concussions -- the fourth time in five seasons that he's missed multiple games. The Bengals are running it back in 2026, so Higgins remains an excellent WR2 who should continue to post reliable WR1/2 numbers when healthy.

Mendoza posted two straight 3,000-yard passing seasons at Cal and Indiana. He displays a strong arm and great mid-range accuracy while showing toughness in the pocket. Mendoza can scramble some, too, which should help his fantasy output. He lacks the upside of recent top QBs like Caleb Williams, but Mendoza has the potential to be a top-10 fantasy QB. Mendoza gets a true centerpiece target in Brock Bowers and a play-caller in Klint Kubiak who has shown the ability to scheme an efficient offense.

Sadiq (6’3, 241) brings an elite athletic profile with the footwork and leaping ability to separate and win at the catch point. He’s a matchup problem, especially in tight coverage, and adds value after the catch. He led Oregon in receptions in 2025, though drops showed up at times. The Jets made him a mid-Round 1 pick, signaling a long-term featured role. Short term, the path to volume looks crowded. Garrett Wilson remains locked in as the top target, and the addition of Omar Cooper Jr. adds another high-percentage option underneath.

The Ravens disappointed in 2025, but Flowers still delivered a career year. He posted personal bests in targets (118), catches (86), and yards (1,211) while finishing as a top-10 fantasy WR for the first time. If Lamar Jackson stays healthy, Flowers has a good chance to repeat that level of production in 2026. Baltimore’s lack of pass-catching depth also points to another heavy role. The one variable is how Flowers is utilized in new OC Declan Doyle’s offense.

2023 turned up an ugly QB situation and poor efficiency on strong volume. Barkley expressed interest in returning to New York but ultimately joined a superior environment in Philly. From there, he enjoyed a historic 2024 with 2,005 yards and 15 total TDs. 2025 marked a clear step back, as Barkley no longer delivered a steady dose of explosive plays. He's signed for three more seasons.

The Jets finished last in passing (140.3 yards per game) and 29th in scoring (17.6 points per game) last season. Predictably, Wilson's production plummeted. Injuries also contributed, as Wilson sprained his knee in Week 6, then re-injured it in Week 11. When he played, Wilson averaged 8.4 targets per game, which wasn't far off from 2024's average of 9.0. Before 2025, Wilson had not missed a game or failed to surpass 140 targets or 1,000 receiving yards in any season. He's still a dynamic talent entering his prime, but landing a legitimate QB will be paramount for Wilson's immediate and long-term value.

Baker's production reached new heights in 2024. Among the highlights: career-best marks in completion rate (71.4%), passing yards (4,500), and passing TDs (41). The addition of Round 1 WR Emeka Egbuka added optimism for 2025, but Baker's play fell off after a strong start. He finished QB12 in total fantasy points -- QB18 in PPG. Mike Evans signed with the 49ers after a 12-year run in Tampa Bay, but the pass-catching group remains in fine shape.

The sophomore slump hit McConkey hard. After posting an impressive 2.59 yards per route run as a rookie, McConeky fell to 1.35 YPRR in Year Two. Keenan Allen also out-targted McConkey in 2025. Allen is a free agent, so there's reason to think McConkey can rebound in 2026. He's still a talented 24-year-old WR, making him a solid buy-low candidate alongside new OC Mike McDaniel.

Injuries impacted Jacobs' effectiveness in 2025, but he still posted back-end RB1 numbers with 16.1 PPR points per game. He'll play next year at 27 -- and with a sizable 1,840 career carries. He's signed through the 2027 season.

Leaning on OC Ben Johnson and an all-star cast, Goff set career highs in completion rate (72.4%) and passing TDs (37) in 2024. Goff's numbers predictably dipped after Johnson left to coach the Bears. Still, the veteran finished a solid QB6 in 17 games. While he'll break in another OC in 2026 -- Drew Petzing -- he'll remain surrounded by plenty of talent. Goff should remain a useful fantasy piece in the short term.

Sam LaPorta had strong volume early in the 2025, but a back inury placed on IR before Thanksgiving. His role in the Lions' offense appears set and he could even be a value if managers have forgotten about his talent due to the missed time. But on an offense with so many weapons, LaPorta won't be as consistent as the top dynasty TEs.

Shough started 2025 as the No. 2 QB behind Spencer Rattler. But the rookie eventually earned the starting gig and played well enough to keep the job into 2026. Shough tallied a 67.6% completion rate, 7.3 yards per attempt, and engineered three game-winning drives for a talent-thin Saints offense. Shough also supplied some rushing upside with a 45-186-3 line. New Orleans upgraded the supporting cast this spring, selecting Arizona State WR Jordyn Tyson at No. 8 overall.

Tate took a backseat to Ohio State phenom Jeremiah Smith but still flashed elite ball skills. He consistently tracked the ball well, beat press coverage, and showed no hesitation working the middle. That skill set, paired with top-five draft capital, points to early production in Tennessee. The depth chart helps, too. Targets are available, and Tate lands with an upside QB in Cam Ward.

Tyson broke out at Arizona State in 2024 with a 74-1,101-10 line. He looked on track for another big season in 2025, scoring 7 TDs in five games before a hamstring injury cut things short. Durability is the concern. His history includes a torn ACL, PCL, and MCL in 2022, plus a broken collarbone in 2024. When healthy, Tyson brings an appealing blend of size, speed, and run-after-catch ability. Those traits give him WR1-level upside. The Saints boosted his outlook by taking him 8th overall. Tyson lands with QB Tyler Shough and could see opportunity open quickly if Chris Olave moves on after his contract year.

Murray was a huge disappointment in 2024, right alongside an underwhelming Marvin Harrison Jr. That trend continued in 2025, this time with the QB missing most of the season with a foot injury. Arizona released Murray in March, but he should find a starting job in free agency.

Lemon might be the best contested-catch WR in this class despite a 5’11, 195-pound frame. He aligned both in the slot and outside at USC, winning the Biletnikoff Award in 2025. His ball tracking and run-after-catch burst make him a dangerous, high-volume PPR weapon. Philadelphia adds to the appeal. The Eagles took him 20th overall, and both GM Howie Roseman and HC Nick Sirianni emphasized his ability to move across the formation. That versatility matters with A.J. Brown expected to be traded. His departure would free up 121 targets.

After a quiet rookie year, Brown showed he belongs as a big piece of Cincy's backfield in 2024. He tallied 229-990-7 rushing -- plus 54-360-4 receiving. Brown notched similar numbers in 2025, despite significant missed time from Joe Burrow. 2026 turns up a contract season for the fourth-year RB.

Burden didn't play much in the first half of his rookie campaign, but starting in Week 10, he flashed fantasy upside. In his final eight games, Burden ranked second in Chicago's WR corps in target share (19%) and led the group in fantasy scoring with 11.4 PPR points per game. With DJ Moore in Buffalo, Burden has a chance to provide WR2 value in 2026 and beyond.

Ward’s college journey took him from Incarnate Word to Washington State to Miami. The top pick improved at each stop, throwing for 4,313 yards, 39 TDs, and seven INTs for the Hurricanes in 2024. Ward showed the ability to extend plays and make accurate throws all over the field. Ultimately, Ward's rookie tape showed extreme highs and lows, but he simply wasn't surrounded by a quality pass-catching group. This offseason brought the arrival of OC Brian Daboll and a slot upgrade in Wan'Dale Robinson -- plus No. 4 overall pick Carnell Tate. Ward should creep closer to QB1 range after Year 2.

Smith topped 1,000 yards for the third time in four seasons, but his target share fell from 28.6% (7th) to 24.3% (17th) in 2025. He also had a career-low four TDs. A.J. Brown's health certainly played a factor in the usage dip. Ultimately, Smith is a good No. 2 WR, but he tends to be erratic week-to-week for a low-volume passing game. If Brown sticks on the roster, Smith will retain a WR2 ceiling in the short term.

Darnold followed up his breakout 2024 with a solid '25. The former third overall pick threw for 4,048 yards, 25 TDs, and 14 INTs with a 67.6% completion rate. He finished QB13 in total fantasy points; QB20 in points per game. We don't see a high ceiling here, but Darnold should supply serviceable fantasy totals in the near term. Seattle retained Rashid Shaheed on a multi-year deal.

Irving performed as one of the most elusive RBs in the 2024 class. From 2022 to 2023, he forced a missed tackle every 2.5 carries – a terrific mark. That elusiveness translated to the pros right away, as he tallied 5.4 yards per carry and 1,513 total yards. 2025 wasn't as friendly, as he missed six games with a foot injury and slotted just 18th in PPR points per game. In 2026, he'll likely see his volume capped by free agent addition Kenneth Gainwell, who posted 73 catches with the Steelers last season.

Brown posted his fourth consecutive season with 1,000-plus yards. He also scored 7 TDs for the third-straight season, but his overall fantasy scoring dropped by two points per game. Offseason reports incidate Brown will eventually get dealt to New England, where he'd retain a No. 1 role alongside Drake Maye.

Tyreek Hill missed most of 2025, but Waddle still could not recapture the production he posted over his first three seasons. Poor QB play drove much of that decline. Now, that aspect of his profile looks better after a March trade to Denver. He projects as a co- No. 1 WR alongside Courtland Sutton.

It took HC Dan Campbell's play-calling to finally unlock Williams, but he showed promise once he became a featured part of Detroit's offense. Under Campbell's playcalling, Williams averaged 16.1 PPR points per game (WR5) and posted three of his four 100-yard performances. Williams remains an elite deep threat, but he also ran the fifth-most routes in '25. He's improved every season and showcased why he should be an important part of Detroit's future. He's signed through the 2029 season.

Kincaid endured another injury-marred season in 2025, missing time with oblique and hamstring injuries. He still flashed upside with two 100-yard games, but never saw more than six targets in any outing despite Buffalo’s shaky WR depth. When healthy, Kincaid can produce as a fantasy starter. But the injury history leaves us skeptical that he'll ever deliver on his high-end TE1 potential.

Gadsden flashed elite fantasy potential early in the season, but it ended with a whimper. Overall, his 47-641-3 stat line is solid for a Round 5 pick who wasn't even active until late September. Los Angeles added TE Charlie Kolar in free agency, but he's expected to be a main contributor in the run game.

Williams further solidified himself as a top RB in 2024, logging 1,481 scrimmage yards and 16 TDs. L.A. spent a Round 3 pick on Blake Corum, but he was lightly used throughout the season. Williams signed an extension in August of '25, but Corum earned a larger piece of the backfield work. Still, Williams delivered 1,252 yards and hit double-digit TDs for the third straight season. We expect similar backfield usage in 2026.

The Cowboys offensive resurgence translated to production from Ferguson. He went 81-595-8 on 100 targets during the fantasy season. He didn't display any amazing athletic skills, but Ferguson's soft hands and good route running made him a reliable option for Dak Prescott. Ferguson signed a four-year extension and is locked in with the Cowboys long term.

Willis has proven effective as a spot-starter for Green Bay over the past two seasons. He peaked in Week 17 of last season with a 36.4-point fantasy effort vs. Baltimore. Now in Miami, Willis has a chance to establish himself as the starter for multiple seasons. The bulk of his fantasy appeal comes from high-end rushing upside, as the supporting cast is barren following the trade of Jaylen Waddle.

Young's rookie year was nothing short of a disaster: 2,877 yards, 11 TDs, and 11 INTs across 16 games. HC Dave Canales arrived to help Young post a promising 2024 – particularly later in the season. From Week 12 on, the 'Bama product ranked sixth in QB fantasy points. His supporting cast received a boost with the arrival of Round 1 WR Tetairoa McMillan in 2025. Still, he underwhelmed for fantasy with a QB18 finish. We're not expecting a major breakout in the fall.

Stafford is under contract through the 2026 season and continued to look excellent in 2025. He finished the regular season with 4,707 yards and a career-high 46 TD passes. He'll play next season at 38 years old.

Stowers is a former QB converted to TE. He will be more slot WR than TE, sitting at 6'4, 235 pounds. He led Vanderbilt with 62 catches last year and was a reliable option for QB Diego Pavia. Stowers has elite explosiveness and short-area quickness, and that was reflected in his excellent Combine performance. Stowers will be the future starting TE for the Eagles. Dallas Goedert is set to be a free agent after this season.

After a dynamic rookie showing, Thomas was one of the most disappointing players in 2025. He missed three games with an ankle injury, but when he did play, he struggled in Liam Coen's offense. Thomas dropped to 45th in target rate (20.4%), had zero games with more than 90 receiving yards, and was tied for worst in the league with 10 drops. Thomas showcased dynamic ability as a rookie and is still only 23, but his dynasty value took a massive hit in 2025.

Price profiles as a power runner with enough burst to rip off long TDs. He ran for 674 yards and 11 scores this past season while working behind stud teammate Jeremiyah Love. His contact balance and short-area agility translate well to the next level. And there’s reason to believe he offers more in the passing game than his 15 career catches suggest. The landing spot boosts his early outlook. Seattle has an opening with Kenneth Walker gone and Zach Charbonnet recovering from a January ACL tear.

Injuries derailed Harrison's sophomore season, but his play also raised serious concerns. Harrison doesn't create separation or create after the catch. He ranked 70th with 1.6 yards after catch per target and dropped to 43rd with 10.5 PPR points per game. Meanwhile, lightly-regarded teammate Michael Wilson thrived with a new QB, planting further doubts about Harrison's future. The Cardinals will also be breaking in a new HC and are likely to part ways with QB Kyler Murray. Harrison has not lived up to his draft billing in his first two seasons and should be considered a risky candidate to rebound in what looks to be a tumultuous third season in Arizona.

Jones' 2023 was derailed by a mid-season ACL tear. Back healthy for a make-or-break 2024, Jones again struggled to prove he's a long-term answer. Then, in a stunning turn, Jones assembled an excellent run in 2025. In 13 games, he racked up 3,101 yards, 19 TDs, 8 INTs, and a 68% completion rate. A torn right Achilles complicates his '26 outlook, but we at least know he'll return to Indy after inking a two-year deal in March.

Simpson threw for 3,567 yards with 28 TDs and 5 INTs at Alabama this past season. He shows solid pocket feel and enough mobility to extend plays, but his inexperience pops up when he holds the ball too long. The Rams made him a surprise pick at No. 13 overall, signaling belief in his long-term upside. Short term, though, this is Matthew Stafford’s job. Simpson’s path to the field hinges on Stafford’s health and timeline.

Through 12 games, Odunze was averaging 12.2 PPR points per game (WR22) with a pair of 100-yard outings and six TDs. But a foot injury resulted in Odunze missing Chicago's last five regular-season games. Still, he was undoubtedly Chicago's top WR in his first season in Ben Johnson's offense, cementing his status as an ascending young WR and dynasty asset.

Skattebo earned plenty of hype as a hard-nosed runner with 1,711 rushing yards in 2024. Arizona State utilized him in the passing game, too, but he didn't display top-notch athleticism. Skattebo did post a strong 39.5" vertical at the NFL Combine. While his Round 4 draft capital wasn't ideal, Skattebo looked like an NFL back in brief 2025 action. He missed most of the season with a serious ankle injury and will push to earn touches for a new staff in 2026.

Injuries derailed Pearsall again in his second season. Having only played 20 of a possible 34 games in two seasons, Pearsall's inability to get on the field remains the biggest concern. When healthy, Pearsall showed promise, topping 85 yards and 13 PPR points in four of nine starts. However, he was also held to fewer than five PPR points in three. If he can ever get -- and stay -- healthy, Pearsall has dynamic WR2 potential. But until he plays a full season, Perasall remains a risk/reward dynasty target.

Barner played ahead of rookie Elijah Arroyo in 2025, but the young athlete may pass Barner sooner rather than later. Barner, only 24, should still remain on dynasty rosters while this situation plays out under new OC Brian Fleury.

Harvey was a surprise second-round pick, but two straight years of 1,400 rushing yards and good receiving skills made him a fit for HC Sean Payton’s offense. His workload increased after J.K. Dobbins sustained a mid-season injury, and the rookie showed well with 12 total scores. Long-term, we don't expect Harvey to become a workhorse. In the short term, he figures to remain in a split backfield alongside Dobbins.

Concepcion built on his NC State flashes with a true breakout at Texas A&M in 2025, finishing 61-919-9 while running 65% of his routes out wide. He added 10 carries for 75 yards and a TD, plus two return scores. He runs crisp routes and presents strong run-after-catch ability. Drops showed up at times, but the usage and playmaking traits should translate. Cleveland brings QB questions, but also a clear path to Year 1 fantasy value. The target tree is thin, with TE Harold Fannin Jr. as the primary competition. That leaves room for Concepcion to earn early volume and emerge as a usable fantasy option right away.

Even at 31, Henry put together a masterful 2024 (325-1,921-16). He brushed off age/decline concerns to drop another strong season in 2025 -- this time with 16 rushing TDs and 5.2 YPC. Henry remains year to year at this stage of his career. We'll see how he meshes with a brand-new coaching staff after the firing of John Harbaugh.

Kittle tallied 57 catches, 628 yards, and 7 TDs across 11 games in 2025. His season ended with a torn right Achilles in the Wild Card Round. It's the type of severe injury that leaves his short-term effectiveness in question. Still, there's at least a chance he returns for Week 1. We'll monitor Kittle's rehab closely.

Coming off his 2024 breakout, injuries derailed McLaurin in 2025. He missed seven games and fell to a career-low 11.4 PPR points per game. His streak of five straight seasons with 110-plus targets and 1,000-plus receiving yards ended, and McLaurin failed to top 100 yards in any of his 10 games. QB Jayden Daniels missed more than half of the season, and promoted QB coach David Blough to replace Kliff Kingsbury. Deebo Samuel is also a free agent, adding to Washington's uncertainty. McLaurin will be back as the team's No. 1 WR in 2026, but in his age-31 season, he should be viewed as a declining dynasty asset.

Williams delivered a bounce-back season with Dallas in 2025. He recorded career highs in carries (252), yards (1,201), yards per carry (4.8), and TDs (11). Williams signed a three-year deal with Dallas ahead of free agency, tying him to the team through the 2028 season.

Watson only played 10 games this season after suffering an ACL tear at the end of last season. Watson ended the season as WR19 in PPR points per game overtaking Matthew Golden. Watson signed a one-year extension keeping him on the Packers in 2026. Watson's athleticism makes him a high upside dynasty stash though his injury history is extensive.

Etienne missed time with a hamstring in 2024 and was outplayed by Tank Bigsby for parts of the season. Come 2025, Etienne watched Bigbsy depart via a September trade. While rookie Bhayshul Tuten replaced Bigsby as a rotational piece, Etienne still delivered the goods in Year 1 under HC Liam Coen (260-1,107-7). The Clemson product landed with the Saints in free agency, giving him a solid OL and an ascending QB in Tyler Shough.

After sitting out three games with a suspension, Addison had two 100-yard performances in his first three games. However, poor QB play torpedoed Minnesota's offense and remains a major concern for the Vikings. Entering his fourth season, Addison remains a boom-or-bust weekly play, and his dynasty value is cloudy until the Vikings stabilize their QB position.

Tua was benched late in the 2025 season, extending a downward trend that began in 2024. Landing with the Falcons allows him a shot at making 2026 starts, especially with Michael Penix Jr. rehabbing an ACL tear. Still, we're unenthusiastic about Tua's dynasty outlook.

Sanders generally underwhelmed across seven late-season starts. The rookie notched a 56.6% completion rate, 6.6 yards per attempt, and a 19.0 QBR. He recorded only one QB1 fantasy finish. Of course, Sanders wasn't surrounded by top talent. Now, we'll see how he's judged by a new coaching staff.

Metcalf set career-low marks in targets (99), catches (59), and yards (850) in his first season in Pittsburgh. He still ranked 22nd with 12.5 PPR points per game and remained one of the best yards-after-catch WRs. Pittsburgh still has QB concerns, which makes Metcalf a somewhat risky option in dynasty, but he is still in his prime at 28.

Higgins only played 54% of Houston's snaps and averaged a paltry 5.4 PPR points per game in his first nine games, but he lived up to his second-round billing in the second half of the season. From Weeks 10-18, Higgins commanded a 17% target share and averaged 9.5 PPR points per game, 25th among all WRs. He's firmly entrenched as Houston's No. 2 WR heading into his sophomore campaign, making him a solid target in dynasty leagues.

A preseason foot fracture limited Likely early and cost him the first four games of the 2025 season. He continued to showcase athletic traits when healthy, but the production was very inconsistent (25-275-1). Likely joined HC John Harbaugh and the Giants in free agency, placing him in a lead TE role ahead of Theo Johnson. There's certainly TE1 potential here, especially if Jaxson Dart shows improvement.

Adams had a marvelous 2025 before going down with a hamstring injury. He sat WR9 in PPR points per game and scored 14 TDs in only 14 games. Adams is signed through 2026 and will run it back with Matthew Stafford for another year. The veteran wideout should help contending teams, but he's certainly nearing the end of his career at 34 (in December).

Pittman matched 2024's 111 targets but was much more productive in '25, snagging 11 more passes and scoring four more TDs. But he fell off down the stretch with QB Philip Rivers under center. In Indianapolis' final four games, Pittman averaged just 7.3 PPR points per game, third among the Colts' WRs. The offseason brought a trade to Pittsburgh, where he'll likely be the No. 2 option alongside DK Metcalf. It's a low-upside spot for immediate fantasy value, and the lack of a long-term answer at QB adds uncertainty.

Pierce had his best season in 2025, going for 47-1003-6. He continued to produce even when Daniel Jones went down with an injury. Somewhat surprisingly, he returned to the Colts on a 4-year deal that includes a stunning $84 million in total guarantees. Michael Pittman's departure sets up Pierce for a larger role.

Strange replaced Evan Engram with success in HC Liam Coen's offense. A quad injury did sideline him for 6 games, but he finished the year with three top-7 TE performances in his last five games. Strange should be locked in as the TE starter going forward for Coen and the Jaguars, making him a solid young TE to roster at only 25 years old.

The sophomore slump hit Worthy hard in 2025. All of his numbers dropped significantly, and once Rashee Rice debuted in Week 7, Worthy failed to score a TD and was held under double-digit PPR points in eight of his final 10 outings. Additionally, QB Patrick Mahomes tore his ACL, and the Chiefs have several pending free agents in their offense. Worthy looks like a good bet to be one of Kansas City's top two WRs next season, but his immediate value is somewhat clouded by all the question marks surrounding the Chiefs.

Evans battled injuries in 2025 and lost his 1,000-yard receiving streak. He will turn 33 before next season, so Evans' career is certainly on the downside. That said, he landed in a fine spot for short-term production with the 49ers. They lost 101 targets via offseason departures.

Sutton ended 2025 as the WR22 in PPR points per game, benefiting from a high-volume passing attack. He remains locked into the X role for HC Sean Payton, especially after signing a new deal that keeps him with the Broncos through 2029. At 31 in October, however, Sutton's likely closer to a decline than he is to his peak. Jaylen Waddle also arrived in an offseason deal, a move that figures to lower Sutton's target volume.

Moore was inconsistent under new HC Ben Johnson. He ended the season going 50-682-6, his worst fantasy output since 2018. GM Ryan Poles then traded Moore to the Bills in March. The 29-year-old should see greater target stability in Buffalo alongside Josh Allen. A WR2 season is within reach for 2026.

Just when Hunter finally had his breakout game (8/101/1) against the Rams, he suffered a knee injury in practice that ultimately ended his rookie campaign. That Week 7 game was his only performance with double-digit fantasy points. 37% of Hunter's snaps came as a CB, which will always be a concern. Additionally, the Jaguars traded for (and extended) Jakboi Meyers and also have Brian Thomas Jr. to syphon targets away from Hunter. Volume, usage, and now, injury, are all concerns for Hunter, making him a risk/reward WR3 in his second season.

Robinson excelled after Malik Nabers' season ended early in 2025. Robinson finished WR17 in PPR points per game and had his second straight season with 140 targets. He signed with Tennessee in free agency, reuniting him with former HC Brian Daboll (now the Titans' OC). Don't expect 140 targets, but volume shouldn't be an issue in a wide-open Tennessee WR corps.

Goedert had a TD-heavy 2025 going 60-591-11. The Eagles schemed up red zone touches for him to score as much as he did, but that may not happen in the future. Goedert is set to be a free agent in 2026, but he is unlikely to find a better situation than the Eagles. He will be 31 heading into the 2026 season and may have peaked in terms of his fantasy output.

Tuten carried the Virginia Tech offense for two years after transferring from North Carolina A&T. He showed excellent speed and vision while adding return value. He posted elite numbers at the NFL Combine as the fastest RB in the 40-yard dash, running a 4.32. He also had the best Vertical (40.5"). We saw his burst appear as a rookie, although he never overtook Travis Etienne as the lead back. There's a chance for a larger role in 2026 with Etienne in New Orleans, but the Jags may opt for a committee with Chris Rodriguez and LeQuint Allen.

Swift's 2023 in Philly proved inconsistent, but he ultimately set career highs in touches and yardage. Chicago provided a fine free agent spot for volume, but a poorly orchestrated offense and a weak O-line limited the fantasy impact. The offensive infrastructure changed alongside new HC Ben Johnson in 2025. As a result, Swift rebounded to finish as the PPR RB14, despite the emergence of Kyle Monangai. The veteran is under contract for one more season.

Najee Harris departed in free agency last spring. But Warren was far from a workhorse after free agent addition Kenneth Gainwell took on a larger-than-expected role. Warren still proved useful with a PPR RB18 finish in 16 games. Gainwell left in free agency, but the Steelers confirmed that they don't want Warren in a workhorse role by signing Rico Dowdle.

Golden had a disappointing rookie season as a first-round WR, finishing 2025 as WR113 in PPR points per game (5.0). Golden couldn't really crack a deep WR group but will get another opportunity in 2026, especially with Romeo Doubs in New England. The second-year pro turns just 23 before next season.

Penix dealt with four season-ending injuries during his time at Indiana (two shoulder injuries and two ACL tears). Healthy at Washington over his final two college seasons, Penix displayed effortless arm strength and excellent decision-making. As a rookie, he displayed a downfield, attacking style in a brief late-season sample. But his 2025 ended with a mid-season ACL tear. While Penix may remain Atlanta's starter in 2026, he's yet to establish himself as the long-term answer.

McCarthy wasn’t asked to carry a Michigan squad led by a strong defense and run game. Still, the two-year starter flashed a power arm and the ability to execute pro-style concepts under HC Jim Harbaugh. 2024 was a developmental year on IR, while the 22-year-old performed as a below-average starter in a limited '25 sample. There's a real chance he's not starting in 2026.

Shakir ended last season as WR35 in PPR, with the Bills' inconsistent passing attack hurting his overall output. He's signed through 2029 and should be locked into the slot role moving forward. The issue is that his ceiling took a hit this offseason with the arrival of D.J. Moore. Based on his contract, the former Bear is likely to stick in Buffalo for at least two seasons.

Doubs continued his high snap counts as a starter in 2025, but the depth of the Packers' pass catchers limited the ceiling. Doubs posted 55-724-6, enough to make him a spot-start WR3/Flex. New England paid up for him in free agency: a 4-year deal with $39 million in total guarantees. He should be the target leader for Drake Maye in 2026.

Cooper led Indiana in receiving in 2025, posting a 64-866-13 line. His body control and strong hands made him a go-to option for Heisman QB Fernando Mendoza, especially in the red zone. He pairs that reliability with strong competitiveness and run-after-catch ability. The Jets traded up to select him 30th overall, making him the WR5 in this class. But the situation adds risk. Garrett Wilson remains locked in as the top target, and the Jets drafted athletic TE Kenyon Sadiq earlier in Round 1. The QB position also lacks a long-term answer.

Seattle made Charbonnet a surprising Round 2 pick, and his usage was predictably limited alongside Kenneth Walker in 2023. A 2024 breakout never truly came, although he showed workhorse potential in games missed by Walker. OC Klink Kubiak favored Charbonnet at the goal line in 2025, leading to a huge 12 rushing scores. A roadblock hit in the playoffs, as he suffered an ACL tear on January 18. His short-term value not only depends on his rehab trajectory but also on the moves Seattle makes in the backfield. So far, they've only added former Packer Emanuel Wilson.

Njoku split time early in the season with rookie Harold Fannin Jr., but the writing was on the wall in the final year of his contract. Njoku eventually slotted behind Fannin and finished the season with 33-293-4. He enters his age-30 season looking for a new team.

Godwin suffered a dislocated ankle that cost him most of the 2024 season. He returned a few weeks into the 2025 season but finished WR54 in PPR points per game. Godwin, now 30, should have a larger role in 2026 with Mike Evans in San Francisco.

Meyers moved from the Raiders to the Jaguars at the 2025 trade deadline, and he instantly excelled in HC Liam Coen's offense. Meyers' success led to the team signing him to a 3-year, $60 million contract extension. We'll see how the team deploys Travis Hunter, but Meyers' deal and production say he's a strong contender to lead this unit in 2026.

Stevenson was held back by a poor O-line and fumbles in 2024. Then in 2025, his workload predictably dipped following the arrival of TreVeyon Henderson. However, the veteran still posted the second-best PPR points per game tally of his career (12.9). Stevenson is under contract for three more seasons.

Andrews signed a three-year extension with the Ravens during the 2025 season, locking him into the team for the foreseeable future. With Isaiah Likely out of town, Andrews should be able to gain TE1 value in the short term. Note that 2026 will be his age-31 season.

Washington was successful when injuries hit the Jaguars' WR corps. Including the postseason, he ended 2025 with four straight games of 9+ targets and 87+ yards. He'd benefit from the Jags making Travis Hunter anything close to a full-time DB. Still, there's starting-caliber talent in the WR room with Jakobi Meyers and Brian Thomas Jr. The Jags signed Meyers to an extension through the 2028 season.
Henry went 55-712-7 during the 2025 fantasy season, living on his ability to score TDs. He turns 32 during a contract season in 2026 but remains a short-term streamer.

Johnson had a career year in 2025, posting 74-828-3 on 97 targets during the fantasy season. With Shough showing promise as the starter, Johnson should retain streaming value in the short term. We're not concerned with the arrival of Noah Fant via free agency.

Hubbard set career highs with 250 carries, 1,195 yards, and 10 TDs in 2024. Carolina rewarded him with a contract extension that November, but 2025 wasn't as fantasy-friendly. New Panther Rico Dowdle became a major backfield piece, limiting Hubbard to an RB38 PPR finish. Just note: Dowdle left for Pittsburgh in free agency. We'll see if Carolina adds another RB to join Hubbard and former Round 2 pick Jonathon Brooks.

Pollard earned the franchise tag in March of '23, then watched Dallas release veteran Zeke Elliott. Still, coming off a January fibula fracture, Pollard took a major step back. Pollard rebounded in 2024, outplaying Tyjae Spears and playing through injuries to post his third straight 1,000-yard season. Pollard closed the '25 season strong but struggled with consistency on a bottom-five offense. 2026 turns up a contract year.

Wilson had a marvelous late-season surge when Marvin Harrison Jr. went down and Jacoby Brissett started at QB. He ended 2025 at WR10 in PPR, going 78-1006-7. We're not doubting the talent here. But the issue is the outlook for Wilson when this offense is healthy. He's also entering a contract season.

Montgomery's rushing yardage (716), TD output (8), and catch total (24) regressed in 2025. This, despite appearing in 17 games for the first time in his career. Ultimately, Montgomery's best fantasy days are behind him. He's signed through 2027.

With the Chiefs' offense taking a step back in 2005, Kelce felt the sting. He finished with 73-839-5 on his fantasy season, his lowest reception total since 2015. In March, we learned that he'd return for his 14th season in Kansas City. He's now year-to-year, making him a shaky dynasty asset.

Corum's rookie year came and went without much fanfare, as Kyren Williams remained a workhorse. Corum also suffered a broken forearm in January of 2025. The good news is that he earned a rotational role last fall, leading to a 145-746-6 rushing line. We expect a similar role in '26.

Reed only played seven games in 2025 due to multiple injuries, so he never broke out for a deep WR group. When active, he tallied only two top-36 fantasy finishes. 2026 turns up a contract season -- and a thinned-out WR corps following the departure of Romeo Doubs.

Boston sat behind the trio of Rome Odunze, Ja'Lynn Polk, and Jalen McMillan in 2023, but the 6'4, 210-pound WR flashed in 2024. He had his best season this year as an X WR, going 63-834-9 on a Huskies team that prioritized the run. He joins the Browns, paired with first-round pick KC Concepcion. Boston should start immediately outside and could turn into the team's top WR.

Johnston proved 2024 wasn't a fluke. He matched his eight TDs and set career-best marks in receiving yards (735) and PPR points per game (12.4). He did so despite ranking just 41st league-wide in target share (19%). We'll see how he fits in new OC Mike McDaniels' offense, but there's room for an expanded role with Keenan Allen likely moving on.

Schultz had one of his best years in 2026, going 78-704-3 during the fantasy season. He posted the most targets of his career at 106. Schultz is locked into the Texans and QB C.J. Stroud for one more season. He should be a suitable low-end TE1 for 2026, but the future is cloudy beyond that.

Spears looked like a 2024 breakout candidate...until Tennessee signed Tony Pollard, who held lead-back duties. That trend continued in '25, although Spears held a clear pass-catching role with 45 catches. 2026 turns up a contract season.

Marks profiled as the ideal receiving back with over 1,400 career receiving yards in college. He is an average athlete, but ran a ton of routes at both Mississippi State and USC. However, as a rookie, Marks caught only 24 passes in 16 games. His rushing volume spiked to 196 attempts, as Nick Chubb remained underwhelming and Joe Mixon never suited up due to a foot injury. Houston added a veteran upgrade in David Montgomery over the winter, capping Marks' short-term ceiling.

Harris struggled to crack the lineup early in his rookie season with Ladd McConkey, Keenan Allen, and Quentin Johnston in front of him. Harris' usage at least increased late in the year, while Allen's departure sets the stage for most consistent usage. Allen leaves behind 122 targets.

Downs flashed again in 2025, but being stuck in the slot role continued to limit his ceiling. Downs ended the season going 58-566-4 with his best games coming with Daniel Jones at QB. With Jones back in Indy and Michael Pittman in Pittsburgh, Downs should have a chance to play in more 2-WR sets. This fall turns up a contract season.

Monangai posted two straight 1,200+ rushing yard seasons for a Rutgers offense that worked through him. His college tape showed a tough runner with a low pad level who works well through contact. Has also flashed on some outside runs. Those traits appeared with the Bears, as he quickly entered a timeshare with D'Andre Swift. Monangai looks poised to retain a rotational role for a young, high-upside offense in 2026.

Mason recorded 4.8 YPC on 159 attempts as a first-year Viking. He was a non-factor in the passing game (14 catches, 51 yards). His short-term upside remains limited with Aaron Jones returning for another season.

2024 marked Dobbins’ return from an Achilles tear. He slowed down after a hot start, ultimately missing four games with an MCL sprain. In Denver, the 26-year-old landed in a fine spot for 2025 opportunity. But he suffered a notable injury again, this time a Lisfranc injury that required surgery. He signed a new deal with the Broncos in March, but durability concerns remain.

Gainwell was lightly used behind Saquon Barkley in 2024. The former Eagle then joined a Pittsburgh squad that watched Najee Harris sign with the Chargers. Surprisingly, Gainwell went on to pace the Steelers with a stunning 73 catches. Now, he'll push Bucky Irving for snaps and touches after signing a two-year deal in March.

McMillan missed most of 2025 with a neck injury, but he returned late to post 7-114-0 against the Dolphins. His short-term outlook improved significantly after Mike Evans signed a multi-year deal in San Francisco.

Okonkwo posted five top-12 fantasy finishes in 2025, his final year in Tennessee. He set career highs in targets (79), catches (56) and yards (560) but remained an underwhelming TD producer (2). He also played all 17 games for the fourth straight season. In Washington, he'll have a chance to boost production for an offense that's moving on from Zach Ertz.
Hockenson's best days are behind him as he heads into his age-29 season. The Vikings at least added talent at QB, signing Kyler Murray after enduring a brutal 2025 under J.J. McCarthy.

A dispute over eligibility limited Croskey-Merritt to only one game in 2024. But he flashed NFL potential in 2023 at New Mexico with 189-1,190-17. Sure enough, he showed it as a rookie. With Brian Robinson and Austin Ekeler out, JCM tallied 805 yards and 8 TDs on 175 attempts. Despite some bright moments, we expect Washington to add a backfield piece this offseason.

Ferguson showed a late-season surge with Davante Adams sidelined. The Rams seem intent on using the young TE as a pseudo WR, which is great for his fantasy potential. Still, uncertainty at QB and a crowded TE depth chart mean dynasty managers will need patience.

Shaheed wasn't a fantasy option after arriving via a mid-season trade. Including the postseason, he topped two catches in only two of 12 games. Seattle showed their faith in him with a new contract: 3 years, $51 million, and nearly $35 million guaranteed. He projects as a volatile WR4 alongside a target hog in Jaxon Smith-Njigba.

Arroyo had a few flashes in his rookie season, but he was never able to rise past A.J. Barner on the depth chart. Arroyo finished the season at 15-179-1, missing the last few games with a knee injury. Still, at only 23, his athletic upside makes him a dynasty hold.

Bryant's snap share picked up as his rookie season wore on. Still, he topped 50 yards only once for an offense that lacked a consistent presence behind Courtland Sutton. Bryant's breakout path is blocked by the addition of Jaylen Waddle, who's signed through the 2028 season.

Allen’s size jumps off the tape: 6’2, 235 pounds. He averaged a robust 3.66 yards after contact per attempt for his college career, plus 5.9 yards per carry and 35 rushing scores. However, Allen didn’t show much as a pass-catcher. One silver lining: The former four-star prospect is still only 22 following a quiet 2024 and a 2025 lost to a knee injury.

Dowdle posted a breakout 235-1,079-2 line with Dallas in 2024. He added 39 catches. That momentum continued with Carolina in 2025. Dowdle posted another 1,000-yard season while outplaying Chuba Hubbard for stretches. That work garnered attention from Pittsburgh, which signed him to a two-year deal in March. He'll enter a rotational role with Jaylen Warren in the short term.

Diggs had a resurgence with the Patriots and Drake Maye in 2025, dropping 85-1013-4 in his age-32 season. But he was released by the team over the winter, as his cap figure and pending legal case sparked the move. He may not sign until the summer.

Aiyuk didn't play in 2025 after an ACL, MCL, and meniscus injury in 2024. The 49ers and Aiyuk may sever ties heading into 2026, and he should be fully healthy on a new squad. Aiyuk already disappointed in 2024 before the injury and is heading into his age-28 season. It appears unlikely he turns into a top-12 fantasy WR again.

A quad injury put Coker on IR to start the 2025 season, but he succeeded when he returned. In 11 games, he went 33-394-3. Coker proved he is an ideal slot WR and played over Xavier Legette upon returning. He also made Adam Thielen expendable. Coker could retain his role going forward and be a fantasy spot-starter.

Otton finished at TE29 in PPR points per game last season. The team showed they valued him with a new three-year deal worth $20 million guaranteed, but that doesn't bolster his fantasy prospects. The 27-year-old remains a low-ceiling dynasty asset.

With Bijan Robinson in the pros, Brooks’ time to shine arrived in 2023. And he did, ranking top-10 nationally in yards after contact per attempt and Pro Football Focus’ elusive rating. He proved explosive as a pass-catcher, too. Just one problem: Brooks’ season ended with a November ACL tear. Then, in December of 2024, Brooks tore the same ACL again. He missed the entire 2025 season but is expected to be ready for training camp. He'll compete for touches alongside Chuba Hubbard.

Sampson is a bit light in the pants, but he moves so fluidly, making him tough to bring down. He had six straight 100+ yard rushing games in his final season for Tennessee. Being paired on the Browns with RB Quinshon Judkins makes Samspon an intriguing third-down and pass-catching back moving forward. Sampson caught 33 of 40 targets as a rookie.

Tracy delivered instant value as a rookie with 1,123 total yards, despite New York providing a terrible supporting cast. The arrow was pointing straight up... until the Giants used a Round 4 pick on bruising RB Cam Skattebo. A season-ending injury to Skattebo allowed Tracy to see more work in '25, but the pair figures to work in a timeshare in 2026.

Franklin enjoyed a mini breakout in 2025. He tallied 65-709-6 for a high-volume passing game led by Bo Nix. Unfortunately, that momentum was halted after the March trade for Jaylen Waddle. Now, Franklin will battle Pat Bryant for the No. 3 WR role.

Mitchell was traded from the Colts to the Jets and immediately made an impact on the weak WR unit. He finished the season with 33-453-2 and may have played his way into a role with the Jets in 2026. He's worth rostering as a dynasty stash. The former Longhorn turns just 24 in October.

Allgeier has proven effective as an early-down thumper, but Bijan Robinson capped the fantasy ceiling in Atlanta. While he’s out of Bijan’s shadow in Arizona, the Cardinals will place him in a similar situation after spending the third overall pick on Jeremiyah Love. Allgeier now projects as more of a handcuff than a standalone fantasy option.
Brissett isn't a long-term solution, but his play down the stretch in 2025 could get him a starting opportunity to open 2026. The veteran turned 33 in December.

Helm flashed at times during his rookie season, though he still sat behind Chig Okonkwo for most of the year. With Okonkwo leaving in free agency, Helm looked to be in a nice spot for spot-start fantasy value in 2026. That still may be the case, but Tennessee added former Giants TE Daniel Bellinger in free agency on a three-year deal with $14 million guaranteed.

A broken right fibula ruined Pacheco's 2024. Even when he returned late in the year, he split work with veteran Kareem Hunt. Pacheco failed to rebound last season but landed in a nice handcuff spot in Detroit.

Kamara set a career low in rushing yards in 2023 (694). He signed a two-year contract extension in October 2024, keeping him under team control through the 2026 season. In '24, he generally outperformed expectations before a late-season groin injury. Another injury surfaced in 2025 -- an MCL sprain -- forcing multiple absences. Now with Travis Etienne in town, Kamara's likely to play elsewhere in the fall.

Bernard followed HC Kalen DeBoer from Washington to Alabama. He had his best season in 2025 at 64-862-7. Bernard has good size and hands as a savvy route runner who works in the slot and outside. He may not have No. 1 WR upside, but Bernard will likely play the slot role for the Steelers. He has a low ceiling, but a high floor.

Freiermuth had an uneven year in 2025 while sharing snaps and targets with Darnell Washington and Jonnu Smith. Freiermuth ended with 38-435-4, good for TE26 in fantasy points per game. There's some hope for a bounce back, as Jonnu Smith left via free agency. Pittsburgh's pass volume should also tick up under new HC Mike McCarthy.

Jennings seized the opportunity with Brandon Aiyuk out all of last season and Ricky Pearsall dealing with injuries. The veteran finished as the WR34 in PPR points per game. We'll see where he ends up in free agency.

We were pumped to see White — an athletic back with pass-catching ability — land in Round 3 in 2022. He managed to get on the field early and eventually work in tandem with Leonard Fournette. 2023 turned up workhorse usage, boosted by a thin backfield and a surprisingly effective Baker Mayfield. However, we baked in some dynasty risk following the 2024 arrival of Bucky Irving, who proved pro-ready. White was largely quiet in 2025 -- despite some missed time from Irving. Washington supplies a nice landing spot, but the team could add more talent via the draft.

Jones battled through injuries to play all 17 games for the Vikings in 2024, while he set a career-high with 1,138 rushing yards. A predictable decline swopped in for 2025, as Jones again faced injuries -- plus poor QB play. He'll turn 32 next season and remains under contract for one more year.

Noel flashed as a rookie when allowed to play in the slot. He went 26-292-2 in a part-time role. The Texans will likely move forward with Noel as the slot WR in 2026, but questions about C.J. Stroud -- plus the presence of Nico Collins and Jayden Higgins -- cloud the upside.

TeSlaa played a unique role in 2025, often serving as a key red zone option. He finished his rookie season going 16-239-6. His playing time increased late in the year and could continue rising in 2026 and beyond. However, he'll be limited by Amon-Ra St. Brown and Jameson Williams for the foreseeable future.

Williams looked like a top WR after he led Clemson in receiving as a true freshman in 2022. But lower body injuries in 2023 derailed him before a 74-904-11 season in 2024. Clemson's weak pass offense hurt Williams in 2025, but he is a versatile WR with good route running and hands. He also lands on a weak Commanders depth chart where Williams can ascend to becoming their No. 2 WR.

Hill dislocated his kneecap and tore multiple ligaments in 2025. At age 31, the injury could signal the end of Hill's career. He will likely try to come back, but it won't be with Miami after he was released in February. 2026 will likely be a slow year after the injury, and he may be too old when fully recovered to still have his elite speed.

Johnson was a surprise in 2025, scoring 5 TDs in his first 9 games of the season. He eventually cooled off, hurt by missed time from Jaxson Dart. But the real issue here is the arrival of TE Isaiah Likely, who joins new HC John Harbaugh from Baltimore. While Johnson remains intriguing from an athleticism perspective, his short-term target outlook takes a hit.

Ayomanor flashed a bit as a rookie in 2025, despite a down year for the Titans' offense. He went 41-515-4 and took over Calvin Ridley's role after Ridley went on IR. The Titans added Wan'Dale Robinson in free agency, and they may invest an early pick on a WR in the draft.

Jeudy struggled to make an impact amidst the terrible Browns' QBs in 2025. He went 50-602-2 in 2025 and didn't look particularly sharp. A new offense (and potentially QB) could help Jeudy get back to contributing for fantasy teams, but the days of him being a top dynasty option are over.

Boutte played a ton of snaps in 2025 and flashed some end zone ability. His 33-551-6 stat line was respectable, but Boutte certainly can be upgraded. If he resumes the same role in 2026, Boutte could. be a spot fantasy starter. If the Patriots add competition, 2025 could have been the peak of Boutte's career.

Horton flashed early as a rookie in 2025, but a shin injury ended his season late (despite the Seahawks making the playoffs). Horton ended the season going 13-161-5. He's an unlikely factor in 2025 after Seattle re-signed Rashid Shaheed. Cooper Kupp remains under contract, too.

In 2025, Parkinson had a strong second half as a red zone target for Matthew Stafford. But the TE will be in a crowded situation next season as he competes with Terrance Ferguson for playing time. Parkinson is under contract for one more season.

Coleman scored 17 total TDs this season as the focal point of the Washington offense. He is on the shorter end, but at 220 pounds, Coleman is a punishing rusher with a low pad level. He is also a strong receiver for his size. Coleman didn't test at the Combine, but nobody will mistake him for a burner. The Broncos could end up being a good landing spot if Coleman can outperform second-year RB RJ Harvey.

Stribling broke out at Washington State before joining Ole Miss in 2025. He went 55-811-6 in the SEC showcasing great hands and after catch ability. Stribling was overdrafted as an early Round 2 pick by the 49ers, but he does offer a vertical element for the team. He could be the No. 3 WR on the 49ers as a rookie.

A tale of two years. Gordon had over 2,000 total yards as a sophomore, flashing great change of direction and vision. He was dangerous in space and used as a receiver in the flat. In his junior season, Gordon couldn’t seem to find any running room and tried to go for the big play too often. His Combine was mediocre, running a 4.61 40-yard dash with pedestrian numbers across the board. Gordon was unspectacular on 70 rookie-year carries behind De'Von Achane. Now, he'll break in a new coaching staff.

Mitchell showed dynamic traits before a December 2023 ACL tear. He missed most of the 2024 season but returned to see part-time work behind Derrick Henry in '25. The results still weren't great: 341 yards and 1 TD on 59 attempts, plus 9 catches for 63 yards. He projects as a part-timer after signing with the Chargers.
Dotson went 18-262-1 on the Eagles in 2025 and still isn't making a fantasy impact. We are unlikely to see him emerge in 2026.

Rodriguez gained a short-term value boost after the August trade of Brian Robinson -- and the September Achilles injury to Austin Ekeler. Across 12 games, he notched 500 yards and 6 TDs on 112 attempts. In March, the Jags signed him to a two-year deal with $6.2 million in guarantees. The contract suggests he'll have a chance to carve out a part-time role alongside Bhayshul Tuten.

Bell has been at Louisville his entire career and has shown improvement every season over his four years. He is a hefty 220 pounds and can box out defenders to make contested catches. He went 72-917-6 last season, including a dominant 9-136-2 game against Miami. Bell snuck into Day 2, though the Dolphins drafted Caleb Douglas before him. Even so, Bell has more fantasy upside than Douglas.

Williams did very little in his rookie season, but there were flashes of being a great deep target. Williams went 10-209-3 in 2025 and should earn a larger role in 2026. But, the Patriots will likely address the position further with young WRs to compete with Williams.

Bech disappointed as a rookie, especially considering the poor WR depth chart for the Raiders. He went 20-224-0 in 2025 and will be paired with a. new coaching staff in 2026. Perhaps Bech can still develop and earn the slot job next season with a rookie QB, so he is worth stashing.

Singleton, a former five-star recruit, has been a disappointment over his four-year career at Penn State. His final rushing line this past year was 123-549-13, mainly profiling as a red zone runner. Singleton would likely have dominated at the NFL Combine, but he suffered a broken bone in his foot at the Senior Bowl. He may not produce much in 2026 in a crowded Titans backfield, but Tony Pollard and Tyjae Spears are set to become free agents after this season.

Samuel had a solid 2025 despite Jayden Daniels missing most of the season. He went 72-727-5 and added 17 rushes for 75 yards and a TD. He will be a free agent heading into 2026 and cross the age-30 mark. Samuel is heading toward the end of his career, but if he can pair with a great QB, Samuel could contribute as a fantasy starter in 2026.

Coleman had plenty of hype heading into 2025, but he failed to pay that off. An 8-112-1 stat line in Week 1 shows Coleman's potential, but he was a healthy scratch for multiple games later in the season. Coleman could get another opportunity in 2026 and should be held on dynasty rosters.

Allar was a potential top-100 pick in the 2025 NFL Draft but opted to go back to school. That led to only 8 TDs and 3 INTs in 6 games before Allar broke his ankle. The physical tools are there for Allar to develop into the Steelers' starting QB in 2027, but the team could still draft a new one in a year. Allar is worth stashing in Superflex.

Dike was a stud returner for the Titans as a rookie in 2025, but he also contributed as a WR. He went 48-423-4 and become the pseudo No. 1 WR on the team. That certainly won't be Dike's role going forward, but he proved he can be a weapon that gets some playing time.

Branch is an intriguing prospect. The former five-star recruit is a jack-of-all-trades at 5'10, 180 pounds. He flashed at USC but transferred to Georgia to have his best season yet. Branch went 81-811-6 for the Dawgs. He also is one of the best returners in the country. Branch lands on a weak Falcons depth chart where he will serve as an offensive weapon immediately.

Neal broke out as a true freshman on a terrible Kansas team, rushing for 707 yards and eight TDs. Since then, he tallied three straight 1,000-yard seasons, including 1,266 yards and 16 TDs in 2024. He also caught at least 20 passes in each of those three seasons. As a rookie, Neal looked just OK in late-season work with Alvin Kamara sidelined. New Orleans then added a clear-cut RB1 in Travis Etienne.

Despite missing several games, Robinson set a career high with 8 rushing scores in 2024. His receiving usage predictably declined, though, while 2025 turned up a move to San Francisco. After playing a handcuff role to CMC, Robinson reached free agency in March.

Allen has four straight seasons of over 860 rushing yards. He has been the consistent backfield presence and ascended past teammate Nicholas Singleton despite a difference in athleticism. Allen is one of the most technically sound RBs in the class, and that shows with him averaging over 5 yards per carry in every college season. He wasn't drafted until the sixth round, but Allen lands in a weak Commanders' RB depth chart.

Johnson was a 2026 surprise. He ran for 1,451 yards and 12 TDs, averaging 5.78 yards per carry. He has also caught 85 passes over the last two years. Johnson can be a PPR difference maker with his pass-catching and shiftiness in the open field. Johnson could make the Chiefs roster and eventually run as a complement to Kenneth Walker.

Washington finally broke out in his final season after transferring from Buffalo to Arkansas. He is a big, between-the-tackles back who churns his legs to pick up extra yards on every run. He showcased good vision and cutback ability, but didn't display much big-play upside. He will likely operate as the backup to Ashton Jeanty for the foreseeable future. HC Klint Kubiak has commented about wanting to utilize two RBs.

Beck's strong end of the season in the college football playoff propped him up to being drafted in the third round. But Beck was volatile throughout the season. Beck has a ton of experience, and strong footwork makes him an ideal backup QB in the NFL. He will get an opportunity to prove himself at some point this season, but Beck is unlikely to stick as the Cardinals long term franchise QB.

Tucker broke out in his third season going 57-696-5. Tucker's 2025 was an improvement in catches, yardage and TDs from 2024. His deep speed made him a downfield option working from the slot. He will have to fend off WRs like Jack Bech and possibly new additions in 2026, but Tucker is a young dynasty stash.

Wicks continued flashing talent in 2025, but he still remains stuck ina. crowded situation for the Packers. Wicks 6-94-2 game in November against the Lions shows his upside, but he may need to leave Green Bay, but he won't be a free agent until after 2026.

Between his leg fracture in 2023 and dislocated kneecap and torn ACL in 2024, Dell did not play at all in 2025. His career is in jeopardy, but if Dell can somehow get back healthy, he could be a sneaky add on deep dynasty rosters.

Despite the addition of rookie Trey Benson, Conner remained the clear starter in 2024. Arizona even signed the veteran to an extension in November of '24, although we'd see his momentum hit a wall in 2025. A September ankle injury cost him the rest of the season and required surgery. We'd bet against a rebound in 2026, especially with the Cardinals adding elite prospect Jeremiyah Love.
